activities.markdown:
# Building Double Bass Speed: The Long Game

Double bass speed is built over years, not months. Drummers who try to rush the process build tension and asymmetry that become increasingly difficult to unlearn.

**The tempo increment method:**
- Set the metronome to a tempo where 16th note double bass feels comfortable and even
- Practice at that tempo for 1 week consistently (20-30 min/day)
- Increase by 4-5 BPM the following week
- If unevenness appears, drop back 10 BPM and rebuild

**The evenness test:** Record yourself from a distance. Play 16th note double bass for 30 seconds. Listen back with a metronome. Every kick should hit exactly on the 16th note grid — no rushing or dragging, and no louder/softer hits between right and left.

**Ankle strength vs. technique:** Many players think double bass speed comes from stronger ankles. In reality, technique (efficient motion, relaxed contact, rebound utilization) contributes more than raw ankle strength. Efficient technique at 100 BPM transfers to 180 BPM; tense 'power playing' at 100 doesn't.

**Progressive overload:** The same progressive overload principle used in strength training applies here. Consistent, incremental challenges produce adaptation. Sporadic maximum-effort sessions produce injury.

activities.title: Double Bass Speed Builder — 16th Notes
activities.description: Continuous 16th note double bass at a controlled tempo. Snare on 2 and 4 for orientation. Focus on: (1) perfect alternation RLRL, (2) identical volume from both feet, (3) zero rushing or dragging. Record and listen critically.

activities.question: What is the primary contributor to double bass speed — raw ankle strength or efficient technique?
choices: Raw ankle strength — stronger muscles move faster
Efficient technique — relaxed motion with rebound utilization transfers better across tempos
Neither — speed comes from pedal equipment alone
activities.explanation: Efficient technique contributes more to double bass speed than raw ankle strength. A player with relaxed, efficient motion that utilizes the pedal's rebound will outpace a player using tense, forceful strokes at nearly every tempo. Technique built at lower speeds transfers upward; tension built at lower speeds doesn't.
